title: Hodge theory for combinatorial projective bundles
abstract: We prove the Hard Lefschetz theorem and Hodge-Riemann relations for certain rings which resemble the cohomology rings of projectivizations of globally generated vector bundles over toric varieties. This proves new cases of the standard conjecture of Hodge type and gives Bloch-Gieseker-type results for tautological classes of matroids.

title: Modularity, Extensions and Connectivity in Infinite Matroids
authors: Mattias Ehatamm, Peter Nelson, Fernanda Rivera Omana
abstract: We generalize the well-studied notion of a modular pair of a finite matroid to arbitrary families of sets in infinite matroids, and use it to develop the theory of infinite matroids in several as-yet-unexplored areas. Our results include a complete theory of single-element extensions, a description of the relationship between quotients and projections, a proof that matroids for which every flat is modular must be finitary, and two new perspectives on the infinite matroid connectivity parameter λ. In most cases, existing theory for finite matroids either fails completely or does not extend in obvious ways, and as a result we develop multiple new techniques for reasoning about infinite matroids, including establishing well-behaved infinite analogues of nullity, local connectivity and skewness. We also point to an online repository containing formalized proofs of all our results using the lean4 proof assistant
ai_summary:
- **Theorem 1 (Single-Element Extensions of Infinite Matroids).**  
  Let $M = (E, \mathcal{I})$ be an infinite matroid. For any element $e \notin E$, there is a complete characterization of the sets of circuits or independent sets that determine a matroid $M'$ on $E \cup \{e\}$ extending $M$, given in terms of modular families of sets.  
  *Plain English:* This theorem generalizes the finite matroid theory of adding a single new element to the ground set, providing necessary and sufficient conditions for when such an extension exists and is well-defined in the infinite setting.

- **Theorem 2 (Quotients and Projections in Infinite Matroids).**  
  For infinite matroids $M$ and $N$ on the same ground set $E$, the relationship between $M$ being a quotient of $N$ (i.e., every flat of $N$ is a flat of $M$) and $N$ being a projection of $M$ (i.e., every flat of $M$ is a flat of $N$ after a certain operation) is fully described by modular pairs of families of flats.  
  *Plain English:* This theorem clarifies how two infinite matroids can be related via quotients and projections, showing that these two notions are dual in a precise sense using modularity of flats.

- **Theorem 3 (Finitariness from Modular Flats).**  
  Let $M$ be an infinite matroid in which every flat is modular (i.e., for every flat $F$ and every set $X$, the rank function satisfies $r(F \cup X) + r(F \cap X) = r(F) + r(X)$). Then $M$ must be finitary (i.e., all circuits are finite).  
  *Plain English:* If an infinite matroid has the property that every flat behaves nicely with respect to the rank function (modularity), then the matroid cannot have any infinite circuits; it is forced to be a finitary matroid.
